title: Organizational Culture & Change
simple_html:
Every organization has a culture — the shared values, beliefs, and "way we do things here" that members learn and pass on. Culture is powerful precisely because it is often invisible: it shapes behavior without anyone announcing the rules. Change is how organizations adapt that culture and their structures to new realities. The two are linked because culture is usually the hardest thing to change — and the biggest reason change efforts fail.

What culture is

Edgar Schein described culture in three layers: artifacts (visible signs — dress, office layout, rituals), espoused values (what the company says it believes), and underlying assumptions (the deep, taken-for-granted beliefs that really drive behavior). Real culture lives in that bottom layer.

Where culture comes from

Culture starts with the founders, is reinforced by who gets hired and promoted, and is transmitted through stories, rituals, symbols, and language. A strong culture creates unity but can also resist needed change.

How change works

Kurt Lewin's classic model has three steps: unfreeze (create motivation to change by challenging the status quo), change (move to the new way), and refreeze (lock in the new behaviors so they stick). John Kotter expanded this into an 8-step process that starts with creating urgency and ends with anchoring changes in the culture.

Resistance is normal

People resist change for understandable reasons: fear of the unknown, loss of security, habit, or distrust of leaders. Good change management addresses these through communication, participation, and support — not force.

Key takeaways

  • Culture = shared values and assumptions; the deepest layer matters most.
  • Founders create culture; hiring, stories, and rituals sustain it.
  • Lewin: unfreeze → change → refreeze; Kotter adds 8 detailed steps.
  • Resistance is natural and informative, not just an obstacle.
  • Manage change with communication, participation, and support.
intermediate_html:
Organizational culture and change are two sides of one coin: culture is the relatively stable system of shared meaning that distinguishes an organization, and change management is the discipline of moving that system — along with structures and behaviors — to a new state. Because culture is the deepest and stickiest organizational layer, it is simultaneously a source of competitive advantage and the primary obstacle to transformation.

Schein's three levels of culture

Edgar Schein's model is the foundational framework:

LevelWhat it isVisibility
ArtifactsVisible structures, dress, rituals, office layout, languageEasy to see, hard to interpret
Espoused valuesStated strategies, goals, philosophiesConscious, declared
Basic underlying assumptionsTaken-for-granted beliefs, perceptions, feelingsInvisible, unconscious — the true essence

Misreading culture from artifacts alone is a classic error; behavior is driven by the underlying assumptions.

How culture forms and is sustained

Culture originates with founders, whose vision and biases imprint the organization. It is sustained through three forces: selection (hiring people who fit), top management (whose actions signal what matters), and socialization (onboarding that teaches newcomers the ropes). It is transmitted through stories, rituals, symbols, and language. A strong culture (widely shared, intensely held values) increases cohesion and reduces turnover but can produce rigidity and groupthink.

Functions and types of culture

Culture provides identity, commitment, stability, and a sense-making/control mechanism. The Competing Values Framework (Cameron & Quinn) maps culture on two axes (internal-external focus; flexibility-control) into four types: Clan (collaborative, family-like), Adhocracy (creative, entrepreneurial), Market (competitive, results-driven), and Hierarchy (structured, controlled). No type is best; fit with strategy and environment determines effectiveness.

Lewin's three-step change model

Kurt Lewin's enduring model: Unfreeze (disrupt the equilibrium and build motivation to change — via increasing driving forces, decreasing restraining forces, or both, per force-field analysis); Change/Move (implement the new behaviors, structures, and processes); Refreeze (institutionalize and stabilize the new state so it becomes the norm). The model's simplicity is its strength; its critique is that modern environments rarely "refreeze."

Kotter's 8-step model

Kotter's process operationalizes Lewin: (1) create urgency, (2) build a guiding coalition, (3) form a vision, (4) communicate the vision, (5) empower broad-based action/remove obstacles, (6) generate short-term wins, (7) consolidate gains and produce more change, (8) anchor changes in the culture. Steps 1-4 roughly map to unfreeze, 5-7 to change, and 8 to refreeze.

Resistance to change

Resistance has individual sources (habit, security, economic fear, fear of the unknown, selective perception) and organizational sources (structural inertia, limited focus of change, group inertia, threats to expertise and to established power relationships). Kotter and Schlesinger's six tactics for overcoming resistance: education/communication, participation, facilitation/support, negotiation, manipulation/co-optation, and (as a last resort) coercion. Participation is powerful because people support what they help create.

Worked mini-case

A 40-year-old manufacturer (strong Hierarchy culture) must become more innovative (shift toward Adhocracy). Leaders who just announce "be innovative" (changing espoused values) fail because the underlying assumptions — rules over experimentation, punishment of failure — remain. Applying Lewin: unfreeze by sharing market-share losses to create urgency; change by piloting cross-functional teams, rewarding intelligent risk-taking, and visibly tolerating smart failures; refreeze by hiring and promoting for the new values and embedding them in appraisal systems. Resistance from senior engineers (threat to expertise/power) is met with participation and reskilling, not coercion.

How to apply it

Diagnose the real culture at the assumptions level, not the artifacts. Use force-field analysis to identify driving and restraining forces. Sequence change with a model (Lewin or Kotter), build urgency and a coalition before acting, secure quick wins, and address resistance with communication and participation. Finally, anchor change by aligning hiring, rewards, and leadership behavior — otherwise the old culture reasserts itself.
full_html:
Organizational culture and change together address the deepest and most consequential challenge in organizational behavior: how a system of shared meaning forms, why it endures, and how it can be deliberately transformed. Culture is, in Schein's (2010) phrase, the "pattern of shared basic assumptions" a group learns as it solves problems of external adaptation and internal integration. Change management is the applied counterpart — the theory and practice of moving organizations from a current to a desired state. The two are inseparable because, as the management adage and empirical record agree, culture is the principal determinant of whether change succeeds; Kotter (1996) estimated roughly 70% of major change efforts fail, most often on cultural grounds.

Defining and layering culture

Schein's (1985, 2010) three-level model remains canonical. Artifacts are visible and audible behavior patterns and structures (dress, layout, rituals, stories) — easy to observe, dangerous to interpret without insider knowledge. Espoused beliefs and values are the consciously articulated strategies, goals, and philosophies. Basic underlying assumptions are the unconscious, taken-for-granted beliefs that ultimately drive perception, thought, and behavior — the true core. The model's diagnostic discipline is to never infer assumptions directly from artifacts; the same open-plan office can encode an assumption of egalitarian collaboration or of surveillance. Hofstede's (1980) national-culture dimensions (power distance, individualism-collectivism, uncertainty avoidance, masculinity-femininity, long-term orientation) supply the societal backdrop within which organizational cultures form, and the GLOBE project (House et al., 2004) extended this to leadership-culture interactions.

Formation, transmission, and strength

Culture begins with founders, whose values and cognitive biases are imprinted through hiring and early decisions (Schein). It is sustained by three mechanisms: selection (attraction-selection-attrition, Schneider 1987, homogenizes membership), top-management action (leaders' behavior signals priorities), and socialization (Van Maanen & Schein's onboarding tactics moving newcomers through pre-arrival, encounter, and metamorphosis). Transmission runs through stories, rituals, material symbols, and language. A strong culture — values widely shared and intensely held — raises behavioral consistency, cohesion, and retention and can substitute for formal control, but it courts rigidity, groupthink (Janis, 1972), and ethical blind spots, and can become a liability when the environment shifts. Sorensen's (2002) work shows strong cultures aid performance in stable environments but impair adaptation in volatile ones.

Typologies of culture

The Competing Values Framework (Quinn & Rohrbaugh, 1983; Cameron & Quinn, 2011) crosses an internal-external focus with a flexibility-control orientation to yield four archetypes: Clan (internal/flexible — collaboration, mentoring), Adhocracy (external/flexible — innovation, agility), Market (external/control — competition, results), and Hierarchy (internal/control — efficiency, standardization). Deal and Kennedy (1982) and Handy's (1985) power/role/task/person typology offer alternatives. The contingency conclusion is constant: cultural effectiveness depends on fit with strategy and environment, not on any single ideal type.

The change imperative and Lewin's model

Drivers of change — technology, competition, economic shocks, demographics, social trends — make adaptation perpetual. Kurt Lewin (1947) gave the field its foundational model and the concept of quasi-stationary equilibrium: behavior is held by a balance of driving and restraining forces (force-field analysis). Change requires three phases: unfreezing (disturbing the equilibrium — ideally by reducing restraining forces rather than merely adding pressure, which provokes resistance), moving (implementing new patterns), and refreezing (re-stabilizing at the new equilibrium). Critics (e.g., proponents of continuous/emergent change) argue that turbulent environments never permit refreezing, and that Lewin's model is too linear and top-down; defenders note it was always a heuristic, not a literal sequence.

Kotter's eight steps and ADKAR

Kotter's (1996) eight-step model operationalizes and corrects common failures: (1) establish a sense of urgency, (2) create a guiding coalition, (3) develop a vision and strategy, (4) communicate the change vision, (5) empower broad-based action by removing obstacles, (6) generate short-term wins, (7) consolidate gains and produce more change, (8) anchor new approaches in the culture. The first four correspond to unfreezing, the middle three to moving, and the last to refreezing — making Kotter a richer elaboration of Lewin rather than a rival. The ADKAR model (Hiatt, 2006) complements these at the individual level: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement — useful because organizational change ultimately requires individual transitions (Bridges' 1991 distinction between situational change and psychological transition is relevant here). A more constructionist alternative, Appreciative Inquiry (Cooperrider & Srivastva, 1987), drives change by amplifying what already works (Discovery-Dream-Design-Destiny) rather than fixing deficits.

Resistance to change

Resistance is the central obstacle and, reframed, a source of information. Individual sources (Robbins & Judge) include habit, security needs, economic fears, fear of the unknown, and selective information processing; organizational sources include structural inertia, limited focus of change (changing one subsystem while others pull back), group inertia, threats to expertise, threats to established power relationships, and threats to resource allocations. Kotter and Schlesinger's (1979) six tactics — education and communication, participation and involvement, facilitation and support, negotiation and agreement, manipulation and co-optation, and explicit/implicit coercion — are arrayed from least to most coercive, with the prescription to choose based on the speed required and the power balance. Participation has the strongest evidence base (Coch & French, 1948) because involvement builds ownership and surfaces legitimate concerns; coercion is fast but breeds latent resentment that undermines refreezing.

Worked example

A regional bank with a dominant Hierarchy culture must pivot to a digital, customer-centric Market/Adhocracy blend. A surface effort — new mission statement and open-plan offices (artifacts and espoused values) — fails because the underlying assumptions (compliance over experimentation, hierarchy over speed) persist. A rigorous program: force-field analysis identifies driving forces (fintech competition, regulatory openness) and restraining forces (legacy IT, risk-averse middle managers, union concerns). Unfreeze/Kotter steps 1-4: leaders dramatize urgency with customer-attrition data, assemble a cross-silo guiding coalition, and over-communicate a concrete vision. Move/steps 5-7: empower agile pilot squads, remove approval bottlenecks, and broadcast early wins (a successful mobile-app launch). Resistance from branch managers (threat to power/expertise) is met with participation and reskilling (Kotter-Schlesinger), not coercion. Refreeze/step 8: rewire hiring, promotion, and incentive systems to the new values, and promote visible exemplars — embedding the change in the assumption layer so it endures.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Mistaking artifacts or espoused values for real culture — "culture change" that never touches underlying assumptions.
  • Assuming a strong culture is always good — ignoring its rigidity and adaptation costs in volatile environments.
  • Skipping the unfreeze phase (urgency) — Kotter's most-cited cause of failure.
  • Treating resistance purely as an obstacle rather than as diagnostic feedback and a participation opportunity.
  • Declaring victory too early (omitting consolidation/refreezing), letting the old culture reassert itself.
  • Over-relying on coercion, which secures compliance but undermines durable institutionalization.

Cross-links

Culture and change synthesize the whole course: Leadership (Lesson 7 — transformational leaders are the prototypical change agents and culture-shapers), Power, Politics & Conflict (Lesson 8 — resistance is largely a power and political phenomenon; change redistributes power), Communication (Lesson 6 — the vehicle for vision and overcoming resistance), Groups & Teams (Lesson 5 — group inertia and guiding coalitions), Motivation (Lessons 3-4 — aligning rewards to refreeze new behavior), and Individual Differences/Perception (Lesson 2 — selective perception and openness shape who resists). As such, this capstone lesson integrates the micro, meso, and macro levels of analysis introduced in Lesson 1.
